June 7, 2026 AI + PMM

The Human Premium

We spent a year building an AI curriculum for product marketers at SAP. What we learned had almost nothing to do with the technology.

Last month I sat in a meeting where a finance leader spent 45 minutes drilling into our AI token consumption. Cost per query. Queries per user. Projected spend at scale. The spreadsheet was immaculate.

What nobody asked: Are we actually getting better at this?

I've been thinking about that meeting ever since, because it captures something broken about how most companies are approaching AI right now. They're treating it like a utility to meter instead of a capability to build. And the gap between those two framings is where all the value is hiding.

The Curriculum

For the past year, my team at SAP has been building something unusual — an eight-unit curriculum that teaches product marketers how to work with AI. Not a lunch-and-learn. Not a prompt engineering cheat sheet. An actual learning program with readings, exercises, labs, and assessments.

We did it because we kept watching the same pattern repeat. Someone would discover Claude or ChatGPT, get excited, produce a few things with it, and then plateau. The output was fine. Faster than before. But it wasn't better. It was just... more.

The marketers who broke through that plateau had something the others didn't, and it wasn't better prompts or fancier tools. It was better foundations. They understood positioning frameworks cold. They had a mental model for buyer psychology. They could tell you why April Dunford's positioning canvas works the way it does, not just fill in the boxes.

When those people used AI, something different happened. The AI became a sparring partner, not a slot machine. They'd push back on its suggestions. Catch its blind spots. Use it to stress-test their thinking instead of outsource it. The output sounded like them, not like everyone else.

"The AI doesn't make you better at positioning. It makes you faster at whatever you already are."

That observation became the thesis of the entire curriculum. AI amplifies capability. If you have strong strategic foundations, AI makes you faster and sharper. If you don't, AI helps you produce mediocrity at scale — and you won't even know it's mediocre, because you don't have the frame of reference to judge.

What the Assessments Showed

We built assessments into every unit. Not quizzes about AI — assessments about the underlying craft. Can you identify weak positioning? Can you spot the gap between a feature description and a value proposition? Can you construct a competitive narrative that doesn't just list capabilities?

The results were humbling. Tool access was nearly universal — everyone had Copilot, most had ChatGPT, some had Claude. But actual capability followed a power law. A small percentage were using AI to genuinely elevate strategic work. The majority were using it as a slightly faster Google.

The gap wasn't about AI fluency. It was about PMM fluency. The people who couldn't construct a good positioning statement by hand also couldn't prompt their way to a good one. The people who understood the frameworks could use AI to explore ten variations in the time it used to take to write one — and they could tell you which three were worth keeping and why.

The Co-Author Problem

Here's what I think most companies are getting wrong. They're framing AI as something you delegate to. Write me a positioning statement. Draft me a product description. Generate some ad copy. It's task completion. It's outsourcing with extra steps.

The marketers who break through are doing something fundamentally different. They're treating AI as something you think with. They don't ask it to write the positioning — they use it to pressure-test their positioning against competitive alternatives, identify gaps in their messaging hierarchy, and challenge assumptions about buyer psychology.

That's not delegation. That's collaboration. And it requires a human who knows enough to hold up their end of the conversation.

I've started calling this the co-author gap. The distance between using AI as a content vending machine and using it as a thought partner. Almost everyone is still on the vending machine side. The ones who cross over are the ones who invested in their own capability first.

The Uncomfortable Math

So back to that finance meeting and the token spreadsheet.

Here's the uncomfortable truth: the constraint isn't the cost of AI. The constraint is the cost of human capability. Every dollar you spend building real skills delivers multiples in AI ROI. Every dollar you save by skipping training gets wasted on tools that people don't know how to use well.

The companies that figure this out will build an advantage that compounds. Their people will get better at using AI, which will make them more productive, which will free them up to learn more, which will make them better at using AI. It's a flywheel, but only if you kick-start it with genuine capability building.

The companies that keep counting tokens will save money on API calls and lose the race entirely.

What We Built

The curriculum is live now — eight units, forty-plus assessments, seven hands-on AI labs. We've had several dozen people go through it so far. The ones who engage seriously come out different. Not because they learned AI tricks, but because they rebuilt their foundations and then discovered what AI can do when you actually know what you're asking for.

We're opening it up more broadly. If you're interested in what a curriculum-based approach to AI enablement looks like — not just for PMMs, but as a model for how to do this kind of capability building — the whole thing is at futureofpmm.com.

The human premium is real. And right now, almost nobody is investing in it.
